featured_quote.text: I use this science-fiction inspired project to undermine stable conceptions of identity and examine how hybrid identities emerge and transform through different kinds of biological and cultural contact.

For me, being young, Asian and female was a poignant identity but people wanted to exoticize and orientalize you. That prompted me to make the video installation Those Fluttering Objects of Desire (1992–93), which was shown at the Whitney Biennial in 1993.
As a young adult, I sought validation—about myself, my heritage, my sense of belonging—from my birth country by seeking some form of authentic self via the Philippines.

If somebody is mixed-race, we assume they’re mixed black and white, not two non-white ethnic groups. Often, I felt like an outsider, and for a long time, creating a visual unity of my cultural heritage was the subject of my work, but as I aged, those concerns receded.

My earlier work is about cultural identification, it took the form of a series of self portraits, still life and portraits over a five year period. The self portraits is called the I-Traits and was based on the photographic idealization and self-imitation of non-white cultural types.

I use this science-fiction inspired project to undermine stable conceptions of identity and examine how hybrid identities emerge and transform through different kinds of biological and cultural contact.

For me, being young, Asian and female was a poignant identity but people wanted to exoticize and orientalize you. That prompted me to make the video installation Those Fluttering Objects of Desire (1992–93), which was shown at the Whitney Biennial in 1993.

To me, it didn’t matter if participants were Hapa in my view or not. Identity is, at its core, a deeply personal and individual decision, and a mandate of this project for me was to never tell anyone else what they were or could be or could not be.

The “afterimage of history” makes me think on a more psychological register about personal patterns. The present conditions one lives in are repetitions, some of which go back to other very early traumatic moments.

It is about self-exploration and discovering and probing the unconscious and intuition. I look at the broken connections, and the erased and forgotten past as the direct result of slavery, indentured servitude, and colonialism.

For me, being young, Asian and female was a poignant identity but people wanted to exoticize and orientalize you. That prompted me to make the video installation Those Fluttering Objects of Desire (1992–93), which was shown at the Whitney Biennial in 1993.

To me, it didn’t matter if participants were Hapa in my view or not. Identity is, at its core, a deeply personal and individual decision, and a mandate of this project for me was to never tell anyone else what they were or could be or could not be.

If somebody is mixed-race, we assume they’re mixed black and white, not two non-white ethnic groups. Often, I felt like an outsider, and for a long time, creating a visual unity of my cultural heritage was the subject of my work, but as I aged, those concerns receded.
As a young adult, I sought validation—about myself, my heritage, my sense of belonging—from my birth country by seeking some form of authentic self via the Philippines.
My artwork is about themes of distance and belonging, and I focus on the fluidity of cultural difference and the slipperiness of identity.

My earlier work is about cultural identification, it took the form of a series of self portraits, still life and portraits over a five year period. The self portraits is called the I-Traits and was based on the photographic idealization and self-imitation of non-white cultural types.

It is about self-exploration and discovering and probing the unconscious and intuition. I look at the broken connections, and the erased and forgotten past as the direct result of slavery, indentured servitude, and colonialism.

It also suggests the life before me, which is the heritage, the history, the culture and the knowledge that has been passed on to me from generation to generation which you cannot visualize. You can’t see it, and you always think you’re an independent person or being.

I hope to wash my subjects of their  ‘otherness’ and reveal them as dignified, even mythic figures on the grander scale of history painting.

Another outcome emerges from the various publics that encounter the works—certain awareness and new perspective—which the artists themselves also gain. Through the Hong Kong chapter of The Vietnam Exodus Project, some of the refugees I’ve connected with are stateless.
By depicting familiar people that are a part of my life, I hope to show a broader idea of who can inhabit art and who creates culture in general.

I would use the funds to complete Unity for Nostalgia, a video work I began last year. This project expands on my ongoing exploration of “ghosts” as metaphors for socio-historical relationships obscured within dominant historical narratives.

I use this science-fiction inspired project to undermine stable conceptions of identity and examine how hybrid identities emerge and transform through different kinds of biological and cultural contact.

Largely related to my unusual ethnic heritage and wanting, needing, to have imagery in the world that reflected my identity, I have always made self-portraits. I turned to the exclusive use of self-portraiture for the figures in my work in 2007.
By depicting familiar people that are a part of my life, I hope to show a broader idea of who can inhabit art and who creates culture in general.

For me, being young, Asian and female was a poignant identity but people wanted to exoticize and orientalize you. That prompted me to make the video installation Those Fluttering Objects of Desire (1992–93), which was shown at the Whitney Biennial in 1993.

I’d already been very grounded in Asian American arts and a collectivized and activist identity but over the course of time I had noticed that more and more of us in Asian American arts were in fact “mixed” and this was something I wanted to explore in my artwork.

On a larger scope, the project has helped push the idea of ethnic identity and the whole concept of multiraciality further into the public discourse and that’s valuable. All these intangibles feed me as an artist and as a teacher and as a person.

I use this science-fiction inspired project to undermine stable conceptions of identity and examine how hybrid identities emerge and transform through different kinds of biological and cultural contact.

It is about self-exploration and discovering and probing the unconscious and intuition. I look at the broken connections, and the erased and forgotten past as the direct result of slavery, indentured servitude, and colonialism.

It also suggests the life before me, which is the heritage, the history, the culture and the knowledge that has been passed on to me from generation to generation which you cannot visualize. You can’t see it, and you always think you’re an independent person or being.

If somebody is mixed-race, we assume they’re mixed black and white, not two non-white ethnic groups. Often, I felt like an outsider, and for a long time, creating a visual unity of my cultural heritage was the subject of my work, but as I aged, those concerns receded.

As a Filipino American raised in the United States, [I find] there is a dearth of representation and also a tendency to cling to visual forms of heritage, even if that representation is found in the heart of the empire.

Maybe some of the people knew of each other, but for me they represent the kind of diversity of that small Filipino community at the time. You know, we have the scholars, we have the people who went to the art schools, we have the self-taught artists.
